sections.heading: EXECUTIVE SUMMARY
sections.title: Executive summary of FSCA's Open Finance policy recommendations
sections.summary: The FSCA outlines seven policy recommendations for Open Finance implementation in South Africa. These include mandatory regulation of participants, licensing requirements, establishing a data-protection framework, developing risk management frameworks, creating data protection standards, ensuring consumer access to information and recourse mechanisms, and forming an Open Finance Advisory Group. The recommendations aim to balance Open Finance benefits with risk mitigation in the financial sector.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes the policy framework for Open Finance in South Africa, requiring financial institutions to implement consent-based data sharing with third parties while managing associated risks. It sets the stage for data transfer impact assessments by defining the regulatory approach to Open Finance.

requirements.text: Participants in Open Finance should be regulated and a mandatory approach implemented
Participants in Open Finance should be licensed and regulated
A suitable data-protection and consent framework must be put in place
Appropriate risk management and disclosure frameworks must be developed
Data protection and sharing standards must be developed
Consumers must have access to information and recourse mechanisms
An Advisory Group for Open Finance comprising industry participants and other experts should be established

sections.source_quote: The FSCA continues to support mandating Open Finance in future, noting that the foundational Open Finance Programme of Work will likely need to take place in preparation to optimise the value to be derived from Open Finance and mimimise market disruption.

sections.heading: BACKGROUND AND INTRODUCTION
sections.title: Background and introduction to Open Finance ecosystem
sections.summary: This section details the FSCA's process for developing Open Finance policy in South Africa, starting from 2020 research and consultations. It highlights the absence of a tailored regulatory framework for financial institutions participating in Open Finance, the risks associated with data sharing including privacy breaches and fraud, and the fact that most third-party providers are unlicensed and outside FSCA's regulatory scope. The section emphasizes the need for a balanced regulatory approach that enables Open Finance while mitigating risks.
sections.dtia_summary: This section identifies key risks in the Open Finance ecosystem that require data transfer impact assessments, particularly the lack of regulatory oversight for third-party providers and the potential for data privacy breaches when financial institutions share customer data.

requirements.text: The FSCA started a comprehensive process in 2020 with the Regulating Open Finance Research and Consultation Paper
The FSCA held two public workshops in 2021 focusing on consent, customer protection, dispute resolution, data-sharing standards, commercial models and data protection
The FSCA developed the Draft Position Paper on Open Finance in 2023
The FSCA conducted an Information Request in October 2023 to gather stakeholder submissions
Most third-party providers are not licensed as financial institutions and fall outside FSCA's regulatory framework
Open Finance offers potential benefits such as increased competition, innovative financial services and enhanced value for customers
Open Finance exposes customers to risks including data privacy breaches, breaches of contractual agreements, and fraud

sections.source_quote: The FSCA continues to support mandating Open Finance in future, noting that the foundational Open Finance Programme of Work will likely need to take place in preparation to optimise the value to be derived from Open Finance and mimimise market disruption.

sections.heading: UNDERSTANDING OPEN FINANCE, OPEN BANKING AND THE DATA
sections.title: Definitions of Open Finance, Open Banking and data sharing ecosystem
sections.summary: This section provides working definitions for Open Finance and Open Finance services, aligning with international standards. It explains that Open Finance is the practice of financial institutions sharing financial customer data with third parties subject to customer consent, and extends beyond Open Banking to include a broader scope of financial products and services. The section also outlines the key entities in the Open Finance ecosystem including data subjects, data holders, data users, and data aggregators.
sections.dtia_summary: This section defines the data sharing ecosystem and key entities involved in Open Finance, which is critical for data transfer impact assessments as it clarifies the roles and responsibilities of different parties in the data flow process.

requirements.text: Open finance means the practice of financial institutions sharing their financial customer's financial data with third parties, subject to that customer's full consent, for the provision of open finance services by those third parties, and any activities connected therewith
Open finance service refers to a service/s developed for a financial customer by using that customer's financial data, which rely on the provision and movement and/or use of that customer's data

sections.source_quote: Open finance means the practice of financial institutions sharing their financial customer's financial data with third parties, subject to that customer's full consent, for the provision of open finance services by those third parties, and any activities connected therewith.

sections.title: Policy considerations and risks for Open Finance implementation
sections.summary: This section outlines policy considerations and risks related to Open Finance in South Africa, including global comparisons with countries that have adopted voluntary or mandatory approaches. It details potential benefits such as improved financial resilience, financial inclusion, and competition. The section also identifies risks across customer, TPP, and financial institution categories, including financial exclusion, privacy concerns, and market competition issues.
sections.dtia_summary: This section is relevant for DTIAs as it identifies key risks and benefits of Open Finance, particularly regarding data sharing mechanisms and consent frameworks that impact cross-border data flows.

requirements.text: FSCA considers global context of voluntary vs mandatory Open Finance approaches
FSCA evaluates South African experience with Open Finance for strategic objectives
FSCA assesses Open Finance benefits including financial resilience, inclusion, and competition
FSCA identifies risks for customers, TPPs, and financial institutions

sections.source_quote: Policy considerations and risks related to Open Finance have been thoroughly interrogated in past IFWG9, FSCA10 and National Payment System Department (NPSD) publications11.

sections.heading: POLICY CONSIDERATIONS AND RISKS
sections.title: Policy considerations and risks for Open Finance implementation

FSCA evaluates South African experience with Open Finance for strategic objectives
FSCA assesses Open Finance benefits including financial resilience, inclusion, and competition

sections.heading: COMMENTS RECEIVED ON DRAFT POSITION PAPER
sections.title: Stakeholder feedback on Open Finance draft position paper
sections.summary: This section details stakeholder comments received on the FSCA's draft Open Finance Position Paper, including preferences for voluntary approaches, customer experience considerations, competitive landscape implications, alignment with existing legislation, liability frameworks, and financial literacy concerns. Stakeholders recommend specific actions such as clarifying regulatory obligations, ensuring alignment with POPIA, and establishing liability frameworks.
sections.dtia_summary: This section is relevant for DTIAs as it provides stakeholder insights into regulatory alignment, consent mechanisms, and risk management requirements for Open Finance implementations.

requirements.text: FSCA may follow up with stakeholders for additional insights on Open Finance
Stakeholders prefer voluntary approach to Open Finance with organic market development
Stakeholders recommend clear regulatory plan for mandatory Open Finance implementation
Stakeholders emphasize customer experience and value should drive Open Finance design
Stakeholders support 'same activity, same risk, same rules' for regulatory oversight
Stakeholders recommend alignment of Open Finance terminology with POPIA
Stakeholders support flexible liability framework for data misuse

sections.source_quote: The FSCA received 16 submissions from stakeholders across different industry segments (see Annexure B).

sections.heading: INFORMATION REQUEST No 2 of 2023
sections.title: Survey of Open Finance activities in South Africa
sections.summary: The FSCA conducted a survey in October 2023 to understand the uptake and practices of Open Finance activities among financial institutions and third-party providers in South Africa. Over 400 submissions were received, revealing that around 50 financial institutions generate more than 5% of revenue from Open Finance activities, with some entities having annual revenues exceeding R1 billion. The survey indicates that payment services are the most popular Open Finance activity, followed by insurance, lending, and KYC services. Financial institutions primarily target the retail market, while some entities focus on the corporate sector. The survey also highlights that most entities use consent management dashboards but have limited API development, with no insurers having developed APIs. The FSCA plans to publish a detailed report on these findings and engage further with stakeholders.
sections.dtia_summary: This section provides context on the current state of Open Finance activities in South Africa, indicating the market's early development stage and the need for consistent frameworks to support responsible data sharing. It highlights the importance of understanding participant practices for effective DTIA design.

requirements.text: Financial institutions and TPPs must submit responses to FSCA's Information Request
FSCA will publish a report detailing findings from the October 2023 Information Request

sections.source_quote: During October 2023, the FSCA surveyed industry participants to garner insights regarding the uptake, usage, and practices by financial institutions and TPPs that participate in Open Finance related activities in South Africa.

sections.heading: FSCA OPEN FINANCE POLICY RECOMMENDATIONS
sections.title: FSCA Policy Recommendations for Open Finance
sections.summary: The FSCA proposes a phased regulatory approach for Open Finance, starting with voluntary adoption and transitioning to mandatory requirements. Key recommendations include regulating participants in Open Finance, implementing tailored oversight, ensuring explicit informed consent, developing risk management frameworks, standardizing data sharing through APIs, and establishing consumer awareness mechanisms. The FSCA emphasizes the need for proportionate regulatory requirements, clear consent principles, and contractual liability allocation for data misuse. The recommendations also include forming an Open Finance Advisory Group to guide implementation and align with international practices.
sections.dtia_summary: This section outlines critical safeguards for DTIA, including explicit consent requirements, risk management frameworks, API standardization, and liability allocation mechanisms that must be considered when assessing cross-border data transfers in Open Finance.

requirements.text: Persons participating in Open Finance should be regulated for safe and ethical sharing of user consent-based financial data
Consent should be explicit, informed, specific, and not conditional on other bundled products
Consent should be clear and distinct, not aggregated with other consent agreements
Customers should freely and voluntarily give and withdraw consent
Consent should not be indefinite and should be easy to withdraw
Providers must not continue to share or utilize data once consent is withdrawn
Consent message must clearly identify risks to consumers
Financial institutions and TPPs must develop risk management and disclosure frameworks
Data sharing should be secure and standardized through agreed API standards
Financial institutions and TPPs may contractually allocate liabilities for data misuse

sections.source_quote: The FSCA aims to promote responsible Open Finance and recommends the following: Persons participating in Open Finance should be regulated for the safe and ethical sharing of user consent-based financial data to TPPs.

sections.heading: PROGRAMME OF WORK GOING FORWARD
sections.title: FSCA Programme of Work for Open Finance
sections.summary: The FSCA outlines a series of actions to advance Open Finance, including developing open API standards, establishing consent and risk management frameworks, promoting voluntary adoption of responsible practices, identifying prioritized use cases for mandatory implementation, assessing legal gaps, building sector awareness, empowering consumers, and creating institutional arrangements between regulators. The programme emphasizes collaboration with stakeholders and the establishment of an Open Finance Advisory Group to guide implementation, with specific timelines for delivering each action item.
sections.dtia_summary: This section details the FSCA's roadmap for Open Finance implementation, highlighting the need for standardized APIs, consent frameworks, and risk management protocols that directly impact DTIA requirements for cross-border data transfers and third-party processing.

requirements.text: FSCA will develop open API standards
FSCA will develop suitable consent, risk management and disclosure frameworks
FSCA will promote voluntary adoption of responsible Open Finance
FSCA will develop a common view of use cases for mandatory implementation
FSCA will assess legal framework for weaknesses and gaps
FSCA will build knowledge and awareness of responsible Open Finance
FSCA will empower consumers to respond to opportunities and risks in Open Finance
FSCA will establish institutional arrangements between regulators

sections.source_quote: The FSCA will hold a series of engagements and workshops with stakeholders to further unpack submissions made to date, in support of the following Programme of Work: Developing open APIs standards.

sections.heading: ANNEXURE A: OPEN FINANCE APPROACH ACROSS VARIOUS JURISDICTIONS
sections.title: Open Finance Approaches Across Jurisdictions
sections.summary: This section compares Open Finance approaches in different jurisdictions, highlighting voluntary models in Singapore, Nigeria, and the US, and mandated models in Brazil, the UK, and the EU. Singapore uses a voluntary approach with standardized APIs and regulatory oversight, Nigeria has a voluntary participation model with tiered data access requirements, the US relies on private sector initiatives, Brazil mandates participation for major banks, and the EU implemented a mandatory framework covering banking and payments with strong regulatory oversight. The section emphasizes the importance of jurisdiction-specific regulatory approaches for data sharing and interoperability.
sections.dtia_summary: This section provides context for DTIA by illustrating jurisdictional differences in Open Finance implementation, which can impact cross-border data transfer compliance and the need for localized regulatory alignment.

requirements.text: Singapore follows a voluntary approach with standardized APIs and regulatory oversight
Nigeria has a voluntary participation model with tiered data access requirements
The US relies on private sector initiatives for Open Banking and APIs
Brazil mandates participation for 12 major banks and payment entities
The EU implemented a mandatory Open Banking framework covering banking and payments
The UK ordered major banks to share customer data with TPPs as a penalty for anticompetitive behavior

sections.source_quote: Singapore follows a voluntary approach to Open Finance with the Monetary Authority of Singapore (MAS) playing an active enabling role.

sections.heading: ANNEXURE C: PROPOSED OPEN FINANCE ROAD MAP
sections.title: Proposed Open Finance Roadmap
sections.summary: The FSCA outlines a detailed timeline for implementing Open Finance initiatives, including establishing an Open Finance Advisory Group by March 2025, developing API standards by March 2026, creating consent and risk management frameworks by September 2026, and conducting workshops with stakeholders by October 2024. The roadmap emphasizes phased implementation, stakeholder collaboration, and the development of infrastructure such as sandbox environments to support responsible data sharing and consumer empowerment.
sections.dtia_summary: This section provides a clear timeline for regulatory actions that directly impact DTIA, including the development of API standards and consent frameworks, which must be considered when assessing cross-border data transfers in the evolving Open Finance ecosystem.

requirements.text: FSCA will establish Open Finance Advisory Group by 31 March 2025
Advisory Group will develop API standards by 31 March 2026
FSCA will develop consent, risk management and disclosure frameworks by 30 September 2026
FSCA will promote voluntary adoption of responsible Open Finance by 31 March 2025
FSCA will develop a common view of use cases for mandatory implementation by 31 March 2025
FSCA will assess legal framework for weaknesses and gaps by 31 March 2026
FSCA will build knowledge and awareness of responsible Open Finance by 31 December 2026
FSCA will empower consumers to respond to opportunities and risks in Open Finance by 31 December 2026

sections.source_quote: Action: Establishing Advisory Group; Owner: FSCA in collaboration with IFWG members; Expected Delivery Date: 31 March 2025
